pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that to improve low fortitude, the most effective reframe is to feel more upset about losing control of one’s behavior than about the triggering event itself. The mechanism is that placing higher negative valence on behavioral loss than on the external cause redirects attention inward and severs the automatic link between feeling and acting out.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi observes that people with low resilience use a bad day as a legitimizing excuse to behave badly toward others — for example, treating a spouse poorly because 'I had a bad day' — which is a failure of self-control rather than an inevitable consequence of the original event.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi distinguishes resilience from fortitude: resilience is the speed of recovery (time to return to baseline), whereas fortitude governs the magnitude of behavioral disruption at the moment of impact. A person can have a severe initial reaction (low fortitude) but return to baseline quickly (high resilience); these are independent variables.

pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that regret is generated by imagining the upside of an unchosen path without accounting for the downside (sacrifice, cost, risk) of that path; this asymmetric mental accounting makes unchosen alternatives appear more attractive than they actually were.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi claims that separating how one feels from how one acts is a sign of behavioral maturity, which is a skill independent of age. The practical implication is that someone can feel terrible internally while behaving normally outwardly, and if no one can observe the internal state, that is a successful execution of high fortitude and high resilience.

pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that cringe is not an accurate social signal about the quality of the cringe-inducing behavior; it is a defensive status play by the observer. When someone says 'that's cringe,' they are signaling that the actor is beginning to shift relative status, which means the actor is on the right path, not the wrong one.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi defines tolerance, the first component of his mental toughness model, as the length of time or amount of hardship a person endures before their behavior changes — not as the absence of pain, but as the duration of maintained intended behavior under stress. A person with high tolerance has a long fuse; a person with low tolerance has a short fuse that anything can trigger.
